title: Ballpoint Ink on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–60 minutes; alcohol cloth tamp, polyester locks ink under heat
stainChemistry: Ballpoint ink is concentrated dye plus oily resin binder plus glycol solvent. The resin holds the dye in place once the ink dries. On polyester, the resin grips the fiber and any heat at any step locks the dye permanently — polyester is industrially dyed with heat, and the same heat at home drives ink dye into the fiber forever. The recurring trap from older home advice is HAIRSPRAY — old hairspray formulas had alcohol that helped, but modern hairsprays are mostly polymer + fragrance and leave a sticky residue that makes the stain worse and harder to clean. The right solvent is straight 91% isopropyl alcohol on a cloth. Working from the back of the fabric with a clean cloth underneath transfers ink out instead of pushing it deeper. The honest cap: ballpoint ink on dyed polyester often plateaus at about 80% removed; a faint shadow can remain.
whyThisWorks: 91% isopropyl alcohol on a cloth dissolves the resin binder and lifts dye onto the cloth — you can see the ink transfer. Working from the back with a clean cloth on the front pushes ink OUT rather than deeper. Dawn cool damp-cloth clears solvent residue. Cool wash + AIR-DRY locks nothing. The discipline that matters: stop if the spot starts SPREADING during alcohol contact (some ink + dyed polyester combinations bleed). Spreading = pro from there.
defaultAssumption: Pen leak in shirt pocket, kid drawing on a polyester garment, signature attempt gone wrong. White or light polyester = high-confidence. Dyed performance polyester = hidden test mandatory. Hot-dried = often permanent.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Don't reach for hairspray — modern formulas are mostly polymer that leaves a sticky residue and makes the stain worse. The right solvent is 91% isopropyl alcohol on a cloth. If the item has been hot-dried with the ink on it, expectations drop sharply — manage outcome.

Inside seam test: dab 91% IPA on a hidden seam, blot with a white cloth. If color transfers, skip alcohol — pro from there. White / light polyester is usually fine; dyed performance polyester sometimes isn't.

spottingProtocol.technique: Transfer pad to push ink OUT
spottingProtocol.equipment: Stack of clean white cloths under the spot
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place a clean white cloth (or stack of paper towels) UNDER the stained area, ink-side facing the cloth. Work from the back. Ink transfers downward into the cloth instead of deeper into the fiber.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 91% IPA on white cotton cloth, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply 91% IPA to a clean white cloth — never pour on the garment. Tamp the back of the stain. Watch the cloth underneath — ink should transfer onto it. Replace the underneath cloth as it picks up ink. Stop if the spot starts SPREADING outward (some dyed polyester bleeds). 2–4 passes for stubborn ink.
Dawn worked into a damp cloth, press the spot 5 min to clear alcohol residue. Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY. Inspect dry.
AIR-DRY only. Inspect in daylight. Faint shadow = repeat alcohol pass before the dryer touches it. NEVER hot dryer until clear — heat locks ink into polyester permanently.

steps: 1. Triage — no hairspray myth, no hot dryer until cleared.
2. Hidden-area IPA test on dyed polyester.
3. Back-side cloth setup as transfer pad.
4. 91% IPA cloth tamp from the back, watch for spreading.
6. AIR-DRY + daylight inspect — never heat until clear.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat locks ink into polyester permanently
warnings: NEVER hairspray — modern formulas leave sticky polymer residue
NEVER hot water or hot dryer until cleared — locks ink permanently
STOP if the spot is SPREADING during alcohol contact — pro from there
Always blot, never rub — rubbing spreads the dye
Hidden-area test on dyed performance polyester before alcohol
Work from BACK with cloth underneath — pushes ink OUT, not deeper

homeSolutions.agent: Skip the Hairspray — It's a Myth Now
homeSolutions.instruction: Older home advice says to spray hairspray on ink stains. That worked when hairsprays were mostly alcohol. Modern hairspray is mostly polymer and fragrance — it leaves a sticky residue that makes the stain WORSE and harder to clean later. Reach for plain 91% isopropyl alcohol instead. Two hard rules on polyester: NO hot water (locks ink) and NO hot dryer until cleared (locks ink permanently).

Before alcohol on dyed performance polyester: dab a drop of 91% IPA on an inside seam, blot with a white cloth. If color transfers, skip alcohol — pro from there. Light or white polyester is usually fine; dyed athletic / performance polyester sometimes isn't.

homeSolutions.agent: Set Up the Cloth Underneath — Transfer Pad
homeSolutions.instruction: Place a stack of clean white paper towels (or cloth) UNDERNEATH the stained area. Work from the BACK of the garment, ink-side facing the cloth. This pushes ink OUT downward rather than deeper into the fiber. The cloth underneath catches the transfer — replace it as it picks up ink.

homeSolutions.agent: 91% Isopropyl Alcohol on a Cloth — Tamp from the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply 91% isopropyl alcohol to a clean white cloth — never pour on the garment. Tamp the BACK of the stain. Watch the cloth underneath — you should see ink transferring onto it. Replace the underneath cloth as it darkens. STOP if the spot starts SPREADING outward — that means the polyester dye is bleeding too, and continuing makes it worse. 2 to 4 passes for stubborn ballpoint.

Dawn worked into a damp cloth, press the spot 5 minutes to clear alcohol residue. Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY. NEVER hot dryer until cleared — heat locks ink into polyester permanently.

homeSolutions.agent: Inspect in Daylight Before Any Heat
homeSolutions.instruction: Air-dry, then inspect in natural daylight before any dryer goes near it. Faint shadow = repeat the alcohol pass before any heat. The honest cap: ballpoint ink on dyed polyester often plateaus at 80% cleared with a faint shadow remaining. If that's where you are after 3 passes, stop — more attempts risk spreading.

steps: 1. NO hairspray. NO hot water. NO hot dryer until cleared.
2. Hidden-area IPA test on dyed polyester.
3. Stack of paper towels UNDER the stain — transfer pad.
4. 91% IPA on cloth, tamp from BACK, watch for spreading.
5. Dawn damp cloth + cool machine wash.
6. AIR-DRY + daylight inspect — never heat until clear.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: STOP if the spot is SPREADING during alcohol contact — that means the polyester dye is bleeding too. Stop on hot-dried ink (often permanent). Stop on dry-clean-only / specialty performance polyester (pro). Stop after 3 passes with no further improvement — risk of spreading.
warnings: NEVER hairspray — sticky polymer residue makes it worse
NEVER hot water or hot dryer until cleared
STOP if spreading — pro from there
Always blot, never rub — rubbing spreads dye
Hidden test on dyed performance polyester
Work from BACK with cloth underneath

neverDo: Never use hairspray — myth, sticky polymer residue
Never hot water on ink + polyester
Never alcohol on dyed performance polyester without hidden test
Never continue if the spot is SPREADING
fiberSensitivities: Dyed performance / athletic polyester: hidden alcohol test mandatory; sometimes pro
Specialty performance fabric (compression, technical, Gore-Tex): pro — coatings react unpredictably
Polyester locks ink under heat — strict cool-only discipline

materialWarnings: Hairspray is the wrong solvent — modern formulas make it worse
Stop if the spot is spreading — escalate, don't push
No hot water / no hot dryer — polyester locks ink
Work from BACK with cloth underneath
Honest cap: faint shadow often remains on dyed polyester

escalation.when: Spot is SPREADING during alcohol contact — pro from there. Hot-dried ink (often permanent). Specialty / performance / dry-clean-only polyester. 3 passes plateau with faint shadow remaining (likely permanent). Hidden-area test shows dye loss before alcohol.
escalation.whatToTell: "Ballpoint ink on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [91% IPA cloth tamp from back + Dawn + cool wash + air-dry]. No hairspray, no hot. [Residual shadow / spreading observed / hot-dried suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with solvent spotting capability

contextQuestions: Fresh leak, hours old, or hot-dried?
Has any other product been tried (hairspray, peroxide)?
Is the spot spreading or holding shape?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The hairspray myth is the most common home failure on ballpoint — modern hairspray polymer makes the stain worse. Spreading during alcohol contact is the early warning that polyester dye is bleeding too — stopping there is the right move. Hot-dried ballpoint on polyester is usually permanent because polyester is industrially dyed with heat. Specialty performance polyester (Gore-Tex, compression, technical) goes to pro because the fabric coatings react unpredictably. Honest cap: 80% removal with a faint shadow is a common outcome on dyed polyester.
